commands.response: `ls` — lists files in the current directory. Use `ls -la` for details including hidden files.
`cd <folder>` — changes directory. `cd ..` goes up one level. `cd ~` goes to home.
`mkdir <name>` — creates a new folder. Use `mkdir -p a/b/c` to create nested folders.
`rm <file>` — deletes a file. `rm -r <folder>` deletes a folder recursively. BE CAREFUL — no undo.
`git init` — initializes a new Git repo in the current directory.
`git add .` — stages all changes. `git add <file>` stages a specific file.
`git commit -m "message"` — commits staged changes with a message.
`git push origin main` — pushes commits to the remote repo.
`git clone <url>` — clones a remote repo to your local machine.
`git status` — shows changed, staged, and untracked files.
`npm install` — installs all packages listed in package.json. Use `npm install <pkg>` for a specific package.
Permission denied usually means you need `sudo` before the command, or the file/folder ownership is wrong. Try `sudo <command>` or `chmod +x <file>` if it's an executable.
'Command not found' means the program isn't installed or isn't in your PATH. Try installing it with `apt install <name>` (Debian/Ubuntu) or `pkg install <name>` (Termux).
The file or directory doesn't exist at that path. Check spelling, use `ls` to see what's there, and confirm you're in the right directory with `pwd`.
`sed -i 's/old/new/g' file.txt` — replaces all occurrences of 'old' with 'new' in file.txt in-place.
`cat <file>` — prints file contents to the terminal.
`pwd` — prints the full path of your current directory.
